From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Planned, socially cohesive, residential community

Several terms redirect here. For other uses, see [Collective settlement (litigation)](/wiki/Collective_settlement_(litigation) "Collective settlement (litigation)"), [Utopian Society (film)](/wiki/Utopian_Society_(film) "Utopian Society (film)"), and [Utopian experiment (disambiguation)](/wiki/Utopian_experiment_(disambiguation) "Utopian experiment (disambiguation)").

For other uses of "Commune" and "Communal", see [Commune (disambiguation)](/wiki/Commune_(disambiguation) "Commune (disambiguation)") and [Communal (disambiguation)](/wiki/Communal_(disambiguation) "Communal (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Planned community](/wiki/Planned_community "Planned community") or [International community](/wiki/International_community "International community").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Peckham_Bruderhof.jpg/250px-Peckham_Bruderhof.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peckham_Bruderhof.jpg)

Members of the [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptist "Anabaptist") Christian [Bruderhof Communities](/wiki/Bruderhof_Communities "Bruderhof Communities") live, eat, work and worship communally.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Amsterdam_-_Young_musicians_-_1250.jpg/250px-Amsterdam_-_Young_musicians_-_1250.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amsterdam_-_Young_musicians_-_1250.jpg)

Young musicians living in a shared community in [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam")

Traditional ashram

An **intentional community** or **commune** is a voluntary residential [community](/wiki/Community "Community") designed to foster a high degree of [social cohesion](/wiki/Group_cohesiveness "Group cohesiveness") and [teamwork](/wiki/Teamwork "Teamwork").[[1]](#cite_note-communal-1)[[2]](#cite_note-pitzer-2)[[3]](#cite_note-mecalf-3) Such communities typically promote shared values or beliefs, or pursue a common vision, which may be [political](/wiki/Political "Political"), [religious](/wiki/Religious "Religious"), [utopian](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia") or [spiritual](/wiki/Spirituality "Spirituality"), or are simply focused on the practical benefits of cooperation and mutual support. While some groups emphasise shared [ideologies](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology"), others are centred on enhancing [social connections](/wiki/Social_connection "Social connection"), sharing resources, and creating meaningful relationships.

Some see intentional communities as [alternative lifestyles](/wiki/Alternative_lifestyles "Alternative lifestyles").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Others see them as impractical [social experiments](/wiki/Social_experiment "Social experiment").[[1]](#cite_note-communal-1)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Some see them as a natural human response to the isolation and fragmentation of modern housing, offering a return to the social bonds and collaborative spirit found in traditional [village](/wiki/Village "Village") life.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Others see them as ways to address problems that are seen as plaguing modern cities, such as alcohol abuse, poverty, unemployment and crime, especially when used in conjunction with emigration from industrialized countries and colonization of new lands.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[The multitude of intentional communities](/wiki/List_of_intentional_communities "List of intentional communities") includes [collective](/wiki/Collective "Collective") households, [cohousing](/wiki/Cohousing "Cohousing") communities, [coliving](/wiki/Coliving "Coliving"), [ecovillages](/wiki/Ecovillage "Ecovillage"), [monasteries](/wiki/Monasteries "Monasteries"), [survivalist retreats](/wiki/Retreat_(survivalism) "Retreat (survivalism)"), [kibbutzim](/wiki/Kibbutz "Kibbutz"), [Hutterite colonies](/wiki/Hutterites "Hutterites"), [ashrams](/wiki/Ashram "Ashram"), and [housing cooperatives](/wiki/Housing_cooperative "Housing cooperative").

As well, [planned developments](/wiki/Planned_communities "Planned communities") such as some [company towns](/wiki/Company_town "Company town") that provided comfortable workers' housing and aspirations of a stable sober workforce, could be considered intentional communities and sometimes even spark from an aspiration for a [utopia](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[Ashrams](/wiki/Ashrams "Ashrams") are likely the earliest intentional communities, founded around 1500 BCE. [Buddhist monasteries](/wiki/Buddhist_monasticism "Buddhist monasticism") appeared around 500 BCE.[[9]](#cite_note-communalidea53-9) [Pythagoras](/wiki/Pythagoras "Pythagoras") founded an intellectual vegetarian commune in about 525 BCE in southern Italy.[[10]](#cite_note-metcalf-10)

Over the last three hundreds years, hundreds of [utopian](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia") communities were formed in Europe, North and South America, Australia, and New Zealand, with the intent to create a better, more sustainable world at least locally.[[10]](#cite_note-metcalf-10) This process is continuing in the modern age, as in the case of [East Wind Community](/wiki/East_Wind_Community "East Wind Community") and [green urbanism](/wiki/Green_urbanism "Green urbanism").

## Synonyms and definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Synonyms and definitions")]

Additional terms referring to an intentional community can be [alternative lifestyle](/wiki/Alternative_lifestyle "Alternative lifestyle"), intentional society, cooperative community, withdrawn community, enacted community, socialist colony, communistic society, collective settlement, communal society, commune, mutualistic community, communitarian experiment, experimental community, utopian experiment, practical utopia, and utopian society.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The term [utopian](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia") community applied to an intentional community might be considered to be pejorative. Many intentional communities do not consider themselves to be utopian.[[1]](#cite_note-communal-1)

The term commune[[a]](#cite_note-13) is considered to be a negative term or even linked to [leftist](/wiki/Leftist "Leftist") politics or [hippies](/wiki/Hippies "Hippies").[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16)

## Variety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Variety")]

The purposes of intentional communities vary and may be political, [spiritual](/wiki/Spirituality "Spirituality"), economic, and environmental.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

There are both [secular](/wiki/Secularity "Secularity") communities and spiritual communities such as Christian intentional communities.[[18]](#cite_note-19) One common practice, particularly in spiritual communities, is [communal meals](/wiki/Communal_meal "Communal meal").[[19]](#cite_note-20)

Members of Christian intentional communities aspire to emulating the practices of the earliest Christians. Using the [biblical](/wiki/Bible "Bible") book of [Acts](/wiki/Acts_of_the_Apostles "Acts of the Apostles") (and, often, the [Sermon on the Mount](/wiki/Sermon_on_the_Mount "Sermon on the Mount")) as a model, these communities strive to demonstrate their faith in a corporate context,[[20]](#cite_note-ic.org-21) and to live out the teachings of the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), practicing compassion and hospitality.[[21]](#cite_note-22) Communities such as the [Simple Way](/wiki/The_Simple_Way "The Simple Way"), the [Bruderhof](/wiki/Bruderhof_Communities "Bruderhof Communities") and [Rutba House](/wiki/Rutba_House "Rutba House") would fall into this category. Despite strict membership criteria, these communities are open to visitors and not reclusive to the extent of some other intentional communities.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

A survey in the 1995 edition of the "[Communities Directory](/wiki/Communities_Directory "Communities Directory")", published by the [Fellowship for Intentional Community](/wiki/Fellowship_for_Intentional_Community "Fellowship for Intentional Community") (FIC), reported that 54 percent of the communities choosing to list themselves were rural, 28 percent were urban, and 10 percent had both rural and urban sites. (Eight percent did not specify.)[[23]](#cite_note-ftc-24)

Some communities are based on [Egalitarian](/wiki/Egalitarianism "Egalitarianism") values. Members have equal access to resources and decision-making, or more broadly individual members "possess equal rights and opportunities, supported by affirmative action".[[24]](#cite_note-Wisdom_Library-25) Egalitarian values can be combined with other values.[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-FEC-27)

[Benjamin Zablocki](/wiki/Benjamin_Zablocki "Benjamin Zablocki") categorized communities this way:[[27]](#cite_note-28)

* Academic communities (see [Living-Learning Communities](/wiki/Learning_community#Living-Learning_Communities "Learning community"))
* Alternative-family communities (see [Tenacious Unicorn Ranch](/wiki/Tenacious_Unicorn_Ranch "Tenacious Unicorn Ranch"))
* [Coliving](/wiki/Coliving "Coliving") communities
* [Cooperative](/wiki/Cooperative "Cooperative") communities
* [Countercultural](/wiki/Countercultural "Countercultural") communities, including the [counterculture of the 1960s](/wiki/Counterculture_of_the_1960s "Counterculture of the 1960s")
* Egalitarian communities
* [Experimental](/wiki/Experimental "Experimental") communities
* [Political](/wiki/Political "Political") communities
* [Psychological](/wiki/Psychological "Psychological") communities (based on [mystical](/wiki/Mystical "Mystical") or [gestalt](/wiki/Gestalt_psychology "Gestalt psychology") principles)
* [Rehabilitational communities](/wiki/Therapeutic_community "Therapeutic community") (see [Synanon](/wiki/Synanon "Synanon"))
* [Religious communities](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery")
* [Spiritual](/wiki/Spirituality "Spirituality") communities

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Governance")]

The most common form of [governance](/wiki/Governance "Governance") in intentional communities is [democratic](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") (64 percent), where decisions are made by some form of [consensus decision-making](/wiki/Consensus_decision-making "Consensus decision-making") or voting.

A [hierarchical](/wiki/Hierarchy "Hierarchy") or [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism") structure governs 9 percent of communities. About the same (11 percent) are a combination of democratic and hierarchical structure. (Sixteen percent did not specify.)[[23]](#cite_note-ftc-24)

## Communes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Communes")]

Communes are a general type of intentional communities.

The central characteristics or core principles of communes were expressed differently over the years. The Suffolk-born radical [John Goodwyn Barmby](/wiki/John_Goodwyn_Barmby "John Goodwyn Barmby") (1820–1881), subsequently a [Unitarian](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") minister, invented the term "[communitarian](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/communitarian "wikt:communitarian")"[[28]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia-29)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] in 1840.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

At the start of the 1970s, *The New Communes* author Ron E. Roberts classified communes as a subclass of a larger category of [utopias](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia").[[30]](#cite_note-31) He listed three main characteristics:[[31]](#cite_note-32)

* First, egalitarianism – communes specifically rejected hierarchy or graduations of social status as being necessary to social order.
* Second, human scale – members of some communes saw the scale of society as it was then organized as being too [industrialized](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation") (or factory sized) and therefore unsympathetic to human dimensions.
* Third, communes were consciously anti-[bureaucratic](/wiki/Bureaucracy "Bureaucracy").

In "Hippies and the Mystic Way: Dropping Out, Unitive Experiences, and Communal Utopianism," Morgan Shipley asserts that by refusing to engage in political debates – even those common among the "New Leftists" within the hippie subculture – hippies were able to create systems of communal responsibility that emphasized "mystical experiences" above all else.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

Twenty-five years later, Dr. Bill Metcalf, in his edited book *Shared Visions, Shared Lives*, defined communes as having the following core principles:[[33]](#cite_note-34)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

* the importance of the group as opposed to the [nuclear family](/wiki/Nuclear_family "Nuclear family") unit
* a "common purse"
* a collective household
* [group decision-making](/wiki/Group_decision-making "Group decision-making") in general and intimate affairs

Hippie communes were characterized by deviation from majority society in "every conceivable way," according to Damon R. Bach's book titled *The American Counterculture: A History of Hippies and Cultural Dissidents*. Bach writes that communes served as a refuge from the values of "mainstream" society, such as consumerism, restrictive social norms, and the military draft.[[34]](#cite_note-:0-35)

Sharing everyday life and facilities, a commune is an idealized form of [family](/wiki/Family "Family"), being a new sort of ["primary group"](/wiki/Primary_and_secondary_groups "Primary and secondary groups") (generally with fewer than 20 people, although there are examples of much larger communes). Commune members have emotional bonds to the whole group rather than to any sub-group,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and the commune is experienced with emotions that go beyond just [social collectivity](/wiki/Social_collectivity "Social collectivity").[[35]](#cite_note-36)

However, Benjamin Zablocki writes in "Problems of Anarchism on Hippie Communes" that tensions could arise between the "original family" of commune members and new arrivals. He cites publicity in the media, sexual jealousies, and changes in leadership structure as potential reasons for these strains.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

The [Fellowship for Intentional Community](/wiki/Fellowship_for_Intentional_Community "Fellowship for Intentional Community") (FIC) defines a commune as an intentional community with 100% income sharing, the Fellowship's online directory[[37]](#cite_note-directory.ic.org-38) lists 222 communes worldwide (28 January 2019).[[38]](#cite_note-FICComunes-39) Some of these are religious institutions such as [abbeys](/wiki/Abbey "Abbey") and [monasteries](/wiki/Monasteries "Monasteries"). Others are based in [anthroposophic](/wiki/Anthroposophic "Anthroposophic") philosophy, including the [Camphill](/wiki/Camphill_Movement "Camphill Movement") villages, which provide support for the education, employment, and daily lives of adults and children with [developmental disabilities](/wiki/Developmental_disability "Developmental disability"), [mental health problems](/wiki/Mental_disorder "Mental disorder") or other [special needs](/wiki/Special_needs "Special needs").[[39]](#cite_note-40)

Many cultures practice natural communal or tribal living, and do not designate their way of life as a planned "commune" per se, though their practices have many characteristics of a commune.

## By country

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: By country")]

See also: [List of intentional communities](/wiki/List_of_intentional_communities "List of intentional communities")

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Australia")]

In [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), many intentional communities started with the hippie movement and those searching for social alternatives to the nuclear family. One of the oldest continuously running communities is called "[Moora Moora](/wiki/Moora_Moora "Moora Moora") Co-operative Community"[[40]](#cite_note-41) with about 47 members (Oct 2021). Located at the top of [Mount Toolebewong](/wiki/Mount_Toolebewong "Mount Toolebewong"), 65 km east of Melbourne, Victoria at an altitude of 600–800 m, this community has been entirely off the electricity grid since its inception in 1974. Founding members still resident include Peter and Sandra Cock.

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Canada")]

Intentional communities were established in Canada as early as the first part of the nineteenth century, and some are in operation in Canada at the present time. An Ontario Quaker sect, [The Children of Peace](/wiki/The_Children_of_Peace "The Children of Peace"), formed a utopian farm settlement at the community of Hope (now Sharon) in East Gwillimbury, York Region, which operated from 1812 to 1889. Other utopian communities were established at [Maxwell](/wiki/Brights_Grove,_Ontario "Brights Grove, Ontario") near Sarnia, and in BC at [Holberg](/wiki/Holberg,_British_Columbia "Holberg, British Columbia") (a [Owenite](/wiki/Owenism "Owenism") settlement founded in 1829), [Ruskin](/wiki/Ruskin,_British_Columbia "Ruskin, British Columbia"), and [Sointula](/wiki/Sointula "Sointula") on Malcolm Island (a well-known historical Canadian utopian settlement).[[41]](#cite_note-42)

Other settlements were established on [temperance](/wiki/Temperance_movement "Temperance movement"), [Henry George](/wiki/Henry_George "Henry George"), [Tolstoyan](/wiki/Tolstoyan_movement "Tolstoyan movement"), [Doukhobor](/wiki/Doukhobors "Doukhobors"), [Orthodox Mennonite](/wiki/Orthodox_Mennonites "Orthodox Mennonites") and [Hutterite](/wiki/Hutterites "Hutterites") principles.[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45)

Canadian utopias also made an appearance on the written page. In the 1897 novel *In the New Capital* by Edmontonian/Torontonian John Galbraith, the main character time-travels from 1897 to 1999 when a new Ottawa is operating under utopian socialist/single tax/temperance laws.[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47) Prairie activist [E.A. Partridge](/wiki/Edward_Alexander_Partridge "Edward Alexander Partridge") discussed the possibilities of a western Canadian utopian [co-operative commonwealth](/wiki/Co-operative_Commonwealth_(society) "Co-operative Commonwealth (society)") called "Coalsamao" in his 1925 book *A war on poverty: the one war that can end war*.[[47]](#cite_note-48) One historian described the 1933 [Regina Manifesto](/wiki/Regina_Manifesto "Regina Manifesto") as at least partly a utopian document.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

[Several intentional settlements](/wiki/List_of_intentional_communities "List of intentional communities") exist today in Canada.

### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Germany")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Rhon_Bruderhof_Building.jpg/250px-Rhon_Bruderhof_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rhon_Bruderhof_Building.jpg)

A building on the Rhön [Bruderhof](/wiki/Bruderhof "Bruderhof")

The first wave of [utopian communities in Germany](/wiki/List_of_German_utopian_communities "List of German utopian communities") began during a period of rapid urbanization between 1890 and 1930. At least about 100 intentional communities are known to have started,[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConti198466-149-50) but data is unreliable.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedemeyer-Kolwe2017132-51) The communities often pursued [nudism](/wiki/Freik%C3%B6rperkultur "Freikörperkultur"), [vegetarian](/wiki/Vegetarianism "Vegetarianism") and [organic](/wiki/Organic_food "Organic food") agriculture, as well as [anabaptism](/wiki/Anabaptism "Anabaptism"), [theosophy](/wiki/Theosophy "Theosophy"), [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism"), [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism"), [eugenics](/wiki/Eugenics "Eugenics") or other religious and political ideologies. Historically, German emigrants were also influential in the creation of intentional communities in other countries, such as the [Bruderhof](/wiki/Bruderhof "Bruderhof") in the United States of America and [Kibbutzim](/wiki/Kibbutz "Kibbutz") in Israel.
In the 1960s, there was a resurgence of communities calling themselves communes, starting with the [Kommune 1](/wiki/Kommune_1 "Kommune 1") in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), without knowledge of or influence by previous movements.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedemeyer-Kolwe2017158-52)
A large number of contemporary intentional communities define themselves as communes, and there is a network of political communes called "Kommuja"[[52]](#cite_note-53) with about 40 member groups (May 2023).

In the German commune book, *Das KommuneBuch*, communes are defined by Elisabeth Voß as communities which:[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVoß199617–26-54)

* Live and work together
* Have a communal economy, i.e., common finances and common property (land, buildings, [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production"))
* Have communal decision making – usually consensus decision making
* Try to reduce hierarchy and hierarchical structures
* Have communalization of housework, childcare and other communal tasks
* Have equality between women and men
* Have low [ecological footprints](/wiki/Ecological_footprint "Ecological footprint") through sharing and saving resources

### Israel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Israel")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/PikiWiki_3560_Architecture_of_the_Golan_Heights.jpg/250px-PikiWiki_3560_Architecture_of_the_Golan_Heights.jpg)](/wiki/File:PikiWiki_3560_Architecture_of_the_Golan_Heights.jpg)

The communal dining hall in [Kibbutz](/wiki/Kibbutz "Kibbutz") [Merom Golan](/wiki/Merom_Golan "Merom Golan"), ca. 1968–1972

[Kibbutzim](/wiki/Kibbutz "Kibbutz") in [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), (sing., kibbutz) are examples of officially organized communes, the first of which were based on agriculture. Other Israeli communities are [Kvutza](/wiki/Kvutza "Kvutza"), [Yishuv Kehilati](/wiki/Community_settlement "Community settlement"), [Moshavim](/wiki/Moshavim "Moshavim") and [Kfar No'ar](/wiki/Youth_village "Youth village"). Today, there are dozens of urban communes growing in the cities of Israel, often called [urban kibbutzim](/wiki/Urban_kibbutz "Urban kibbutz"). The urban kibbutzim are smaller and more [anarchist](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism").[[54]](#cite_note-55) Most of the urban communes in Israel emphasize social change, education, and local involvement in the cities where they live. Some of the urban communes have members who are graduates of [zionist](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism")-[socialist](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") youth movements, like [HaNoar HaOved VeHaLomed](/wiki/HaNoar_HaOved_VeHaLomed "HaNoar HaOved VeHaLomed"), [HaMahanot HaOlim](/wiki/HaMahanot_HaOlim "HaMahanot HaOlim") and [Hashomer Hatsair](/wiki/Hashomer_Hatsair "Hashomer Hatsair").[[55]](#cite_note-56)

### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Ireland")]

In 1831 John Vandeleur (a landlord) established a commune on his [Ralahine Estate](/wiki/Ralahine "Ralahine") at [Newmarket-on-Fergus](/wiki/Newmarket-on-Fergus "Newmarket-on-Fergus"), [County Clare](/wiki/County_Clare "County Clare"). Vandeleur asked Edward Thomas Craig, an English socialist, to formulate rules and regulations for the commune. It was set up with a population of 22 adult single men, 7 married women and their 7 husbands, 5 single women, 4 orphan boys and 5 children under the age of 9 years. No money was employed, only credit notes could be used in the commune shop. All occupants were committed to a life with no alcohol, tobacco, snuff or gambling. All were required to work for 12 hours a day during the summer and from dawn to dusk in winter. The social experiment prospered for a time, and 29 new members joined.

However, in 1833 the experiment collapsed due to the gambling debts of John Vandeleur. The members of the commune met for the last time on 23 November 1833 and placed on record a declaration of "the contentment, peace and happiness they had experienced for two years under the arrangements introduced by Mr. Vandeleur and Mr. Craig and which through no fault of the Association was now at an end".[[56]](#cite_note-57)

### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Mexico")]

[Topolobampo](/wiki/Topolobampo#Topolobampo_utopian_colony "Topolobampo") was the site of an utopian colony from 1886 to 1893. U.S. author [Marie Howland](/wiki/Marie_Howland "Marie Howland") and Canadian book publisher John W. Lovell were prominent in the community.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Russia")]

In [imperial Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), the vast majority of Russian peasants held their land in communal ownership within a [*mir*](/wiki/Mir_(commune) "Mir (commune)") community, which acted as a village government and a cooperative.[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60) The very widespread and influential pre-Soviet Russian tradition of monastic communities of both sexes could also be considered a form of communal living. After the [end of communism in Russia](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union"), [monastic communities](/wiki/Monastic_communities "Monastic communities") have again become more common, populous and, to a lesser degree, more influential in Russian society. Various patterns of Russian behavior — *toloka* (толока), *pomochi* (помочи), *[artel](/wiki/Artel "Artel")* (артель) — are also based on communal ("мирские") traditions.

In the years immediately following the [revolutions of 1917](/wiki/Russian_Revolution_(1917) "Russian Revolution (1917)") [Tolstoyan](/wiki/Tolstoyan_movement "Tolstoyan movement") communities proliferated in Russia, but they were eventually wiped out or stripped of their independence due to [collectivisation](/wiki/Collectivization_in_the_Soviet_Union "Collectivization in the Soviet Union") and ideological purges in the late 1920s.[[60]](#cite_note-61) Colonies, such as the [Life and Labor Commune](/wiki/Life_and_Labor_Commune "Life and Labor Commune"), relocated to [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") to avoid being liquidated. Several Tolstoyan leaders, including [Yakov Dragunovsky](/w/index.php?title=Yakov_Dragunovsky&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yakov Dragunovsky (page does not exist)") (1886–1937), were put on trial and then sent to the [Gulag](/wiki/Gulag "Gulag") prison camps.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

Some Tolstoyans emigrated to Canada.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

### South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: South Africa")]

In 1991, [Afrikaners](/wiki/Afrikaners "Afrikaners") in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") founded the controversial Afrikaner-only town of [Orania](/wiki/Orania "Orania"), with the goal of creating a stronghold for the Afrikaner minority group, the Afrikaans language and the Afrikaner culture.[[63]](#cite_note-64) By 2022, the population was 2,500. The town was experiencing rapid growth and the population had climbed by 55% from 2018.[[64]](#cite_note-65) They favour a model of strict Afrikaner self-sufficiency and have their own currency, bank and local government, and only employ Afrikaners.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Findhorn_wind_turbines.jpg/250px-Findhorn_wind_turbines.jpg)](/wiki/File:Findhorn_wind_turbines.jpg)

The wind turbines at Findhorn make the Ecovillage a net exporter of electricity.

A 19th century advocate and practitioner of communal living was the [utopian socialist](/wiki/Utopian_socialism "Utopian socialism") [John Goodwyn Barmby](/wiki/John_Goodwyn_Barmby "John Goodwyn Barmby"), who founded a Communist Church before becoming a [Unitarian](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") minister.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

[The Simon Community](/wiki/The_Simon_Community "The Simon Community") in [London](/wiki/London "London") is an example of social cooperation, made to ease [homelessness](/wiki/Homelessness_in_the_United_Kingdom "Homelessness in the United Kingdom") within London. It provides food and religion and is staffed by homeless people and volunteers.[[67]](#cite_note-The_Simon_Community-68) Mildly nomadic, they run street "cafés" which distribute food to their known members and to the general public.

The [Bruderhof](/wiki/Bruderhof "Bruderhof")[[68]](#cite_note-69) has three locations in the UK.[[69]](#cite_note-70) In [Glandwr](/wiki/Glandwr,_Pembrokeshire "Glandwr, Pembrokeshire"), near [Crymych](/wiki/Crymych "Crymych"), [Pembrokeshire](/wiki/Pembrokeshire "Pembrokeshire"), a co-op called [Lammas Ecovillage](/wiki/Lammas_Ecovillage "Lammas Ecovillage") focuses on planning and [sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development"). Granted planning permission by the [Welsh Government](/wiki/Welsh_Government "Welsh Government") in 2009, it has since created 9 holdings and is a central communal hub for its community.[[70]](#cite_note-71) In [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), the [Findhorn Foundation](/wiki/Findhorn_Foundation "Findhorn Foundation") founded by [Peter](/wiki/Peter_Caddy "Peter Caddy") and [Eileen Caddy](/wiki/Eileen_Caddy "Eileen Caddy") and [Dorothy Maclean](/wiki/Dorothy_Maclean "Dorothy Maclean") in 1962[[71]](#cite_note-Findhorn_Foundation-72) is prominent for its educational centre and experimental architectural community project based at The Park, in [Moray](/wiki/Moray "Moray"), Scotland, near the village of [Findhorn](/wiki/Findhorn "Findhorn").[[72]](#cite_note-local-73)

The [Findhorn Ecovillage](/wiki/Findhorn_Ecovillage "Findhorn Ecovillage") community at The Park, Findhorn, a village in Moray, Scotland, and at [Cluny Hill](/wiki/Cluny_Hill "Cluny Hill") in [Forres](/wiki/Forres "Forres"), now houses more than 400 people.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

Historic [agricultural examples](/wiki/Agricultural_commune "Agricultural commune") include the [Diggers](/wiki/Diggers "Diggers") settlement on [St George's Hill](/wiki/St_George%27s_Hill "St George's Hill"), Surrey during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") and the [Clousden Hill Free Communist and Co-operative Colony](/wiki/Clousden_Hill_Free_Communist_and_Co-operative_Colony "Clousden Hill Free Communist and Co-operative Colony") near [Newcastle upon Tyne](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne") during the 1890s.[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Intentional_community&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: United States")]

A variety of alternative living arrangements, based on aspirations for better living and relief from burden of consumerism and insobriety, dot U.S. history, as demonstrated by attempts, at the large and small scale, to establish intentional communities during the long course of that country's history. Although some communities were established just to help its residents get through poor economic times, such as Depression-era [government-sponsored subsistence homesteads](/wiki/Subsistence_Homesteads_Division "Subsistence Homesteads Division"), [others](/wiki/List_of_American_utopian_communities "List of American utopian communities") pursued socialist, anarchist, free-love or other utopian schemes.[[76]](#cite_note-77) Even the many [company towns](/wiki/Company_towns "Company towns") in the U.S. in the early 1900s could be considered part of this story.
These historic [utopian communities](/wiki/List_of_American_utopian_communities "List of American utopian communities") predated and led to the rise of the communes of the [hippie](/wiki/Hippie "Hippie") movement and the "[back-to-the-land](/wiki/Back-to-the-land "Back-to-the-land")" ventures of the 1960s and 1970s.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

A commune that played a large role in the hippie movement was [Kaliflower](/wiki/Kaliflower_Commune "Kaliflower Commune"). This utopian living cooperative started in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") in 1967 with the values of [free love](/wiki/Free_love "Free love") and [anti-capitalism](/wiki/Anti-capitalism "Anti-capitalism"). Two other prominent communes in northern California at the time were Wheeler's Ranch and Morning Star Ranch.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

The majority of residents in American hippie communes were white, upper-middle class young people, according to Gilbert Zicklin's 1983 book *Countercultural Communes: A Sociological Perspective*.[[79]](#cite_note-80) Damon Bach writes that oftentimes, minorities and low-income individuals were hesitant to join communes because they were unlikely to accept voluntary poverty.[[34]](#cite_note-:0-35)

[Andrew Jacobs](/wiki/Andrew_Jacobs_(journalist) "Andrew Jacobs (journalist)") of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* wrote in 2006 that "after decades of contraction, the American commune movement has been expanding since the mid-1990s, spurred by the growth of settlements that seek to marry the utopian-minded commune of the 1960s with the American predilection for privacy and capital appreciation".[[80]](#cite_note-NYTimes_2006-06-11-81) The [Fellowship for Intentional Community](/wiki/Fellowship_for_Intentional_Community "Fellowship for Intentional Community") (FIC) is one of the main sources for listings of and more information about communes in the United States.

Although many American communes are short-lived, some have been in operation for over 50 years. The [Bruderhof](/wiki/Bruderhof_Communities "Bruderhof Communities") was established in the US in 1954,[[20]](#cite_note-ic.org-21) [Twin Oaks](/wiki/Twin_Oaks_Community,_Virginia "Twin Oaks Community, Virginia") in 1967[[81]](#cite_note-82) and [Koinonia Farm](/wiki/Koinonia_Partners "Koinonia Partners") in 1942.[[82]](#cite_note-83) Twin Oaks is a rare example of a non-religious commune surviving for longer than 30 years. A newer intentional community is [Synchronicity LA](/wiki/Synchronicity_LA "Synchronicity LA"), founded in 2008.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The phenomena of protest camps, some as recently as [2020](/wiki/Capitol_Hill_Occupied_Protest "Capitol Hill Occupied Protest"), may be considered part of the history of U.S. intentional communities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [freedom colonies](/wiki/Freedmen%27s_town "Freedmen's town") were enclaves of freed slaves who sought to live without racist attacks.
